title: Say that it is a list
why.no-ul: There is still no list. Wrap the four things in <ul> — that says "everything inside is of one kind".
why.no-li: The wrapper is there, but there is nothing inside it. Each thing gets wrapped in <li>.
why.manual-dashes: You left the dashes inside the <li>. You do not need them — the browser draws the bullets. Your dashes are now sitting NEXT TO its bullets.
why.old-mess: The old paragraph with the dashes is still there. Delete it — you have a real list now.
why.not-closed:
A tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order. In lists this happens easily — every
  • wants its
    • .
blocks.0.text: The problem from the last lesson: you drew a list instead of making one. Here is the tool.
blocks.3.text: <ul> wraps the whole list. It comes from "unordered list". Meaning the order does not matter: the frame is not "more first" than the bell.
blocks.4.text: <li> is each thing inside. From "list item".
blocks.6.text: The dashes are gone. You did not type a single bullet — the browser draws them itself. You only said what this IS; how it looks, it decided.
blocks.7.text:
And there is no
. Every
  • is a block — it takes its own line. It does not need to be told.
blocks.8.text: And if the order does matter
blocks.9.text: Then it is not <ul> but <ol> — "ordered list". Steps in a recipe, a ranking, instructions. The browser numbers them itself, and if you swap two — the numbers rearrange on their own. Try that with hand-typed digits.
